Metro Vancouver.climate: Mild oceanic coastal climate: wet, mild winters (rain far more often than snow at sea level), warm dry summers; heavy November-March rainfall drives moisture, drainage and moss/algae issues on building exteriors; freeze-thaw is rare at sea level but real on the North Shore slopes.
Metro Vancouver.terrain: Delta and floodplain of the Fraser River meeting the Coast Mountains and the Salish Sea; North Shore municipalities climb steep forested mountain slopes; Richmond and Delta sit on low-lying river delta soils.
Metro Vancouver.economy: Canada's third-largest metropolitan economy: Port of Vancouver (Canada's largest port), film/TV production, tech, finance, construction and real estate; major institutions include UBC, SFU, Vancouver International Airport (YVR) in Richmond.
Metro Vancouver.building_stock: The widest mix in BC: pre-war character homes and Vancouver Specials, leaky-condo-era mid-rises now rehabilitated, glass high-rise towers, and large new master-planned suburbs (Surrey, Langley); strata-governed multi-family housing is a defining feature.

Vancouver Island South.climate: The mildest climate in Canada: sub-Mediterranean pattern with dry summers and wet winters; Victoria sits in the Olympic Mountains rain shadow with roughly half Vancouver's rainfall; ocean salt air affects exposed exteriors.
Vancouver Island South.terrain: Rocky Salish Sea coastline, Garry oak meadows, and the Saanich Peninsula's farmland; the Malahat ridge separates Greater Victoria from the Cowichan Valley.
Vancouver Island South.economy: Provincial government (Victoria is BC's capital), CFB Esquimalt (Pacific naval base), University of Victoria, tourism, marine industries, and tech ('Tectoria').
Vancouver Island South.building_stock: Canada's oldest housing stock profile by share of pre-1960 homes: heritage Victorian and Edwardian houses, post-war bungalows in Saanich, condo towers in the core, and acreage properties up the peninsula.

Fraser Valley.climate: Coastal climate with more temperature range than Metro Vancouver: heavier winter rain, occasional outflow freezes and snow events, hot summers; the November 2021 atmospheric-river flooding of the Sumas Prairie made valley drainage a household topic.
Fraser Valley.terrain: Broad, fertile Fraser River floodplain framed by the Cascade and Coast mountains; Sumas Prairie reclaimed lakebed; steep slopes above Chilliwack and Hope.
Fraser Valley.economy: BC's agricultural heartland: dairy, berries (Abbotsford calls itself the Raspberry Capital of Canada), poultry; plus aerospace at Abbotsford International Airport, manufacturing, and rapid residential growth along Highway 1.
Fraser Valley.building_stock: Farmhouses and rural acreages, big post-2000 suburban subdivisions in Abbotsford and Chilliwack, and new multi-family districts around urban centres; many properties on well and septic outside city cores.

Vancouver Island Central & North.climate: Wet coastal temperate rainforest climate, wetter and windier moving north and to the outer coast; Tofino/Ucluelet face open-Pacific storms (storm-watching season); inland east-coast communities like the Comox Valley are drier and sunnier.
Vancouver Island Central & North.terrain: The Vancouver Island Ranges spine, old-growth rainforest, long fjords and inlets, and the sheltered east-coast plain along the Strait of Georgia where most communities sit.
Vancouver Island Central & North.economy: Forestry heritage transitioning to aquaculture, tourism, retirees and remote work; Nanaimo is the island's transport hub (two ferry terminals to the mainland); Comox hosts CFB Comox; Port Alberni, Campbell River and Port Hardy anchor resource industries.
Vancouver Island Central & North.building_stock: Post-war mill-town houses, 1970s-90s ranchers, waterfront and view homes, and fast-growing retirement-driven subdivisions in Nanaimo, Parksville-Qualicum and the Comox Valley.

Gulf Islands & Sunshine Coast.climate: Rain-shadow mildness on the southern Gulf Islands (among Canada's sunniest coastal spots); the Sunshine Coast lives up to its name with drier summers than Vancouver but still wet winters; everything is marine-exposed.
Gulf Islands & Sunshine Coast.terrain: An archipelago of forested islands in the Salish Sea and a mainland coast strip (the Sunshine Coast) reachable only by ferry or air despite being on the mainland; rocky shorelines, ferry-dependent logistics.
Gulf Islands & Sunshine Coast.economy: Tourism, arts communities, retirees, small-scale agriculture and aquaculture; ferry schedules shape all commerce; Salt Spring Island's Saturday Market and Powell River's converted mill economy are regional anchors.
Gulf Islands & Sunshine Coast.building_stock: Cottages and character homes, high-value waterfront properties, off-grid and rural systems (wells, septic), and older housing needing marine-climate upkeep; island logistics raise every project's complexity.

Sea-to-Sky.climate: Steep climate gradient in 120 km: mild wet coastal at Squamish to alpine snow country at Whistler; heavy winter snow loads and freeze-thaw at elevation; intense short building seasons up-corridor.
Sea-to-Sky.terrain: The Sea-to-Sky Highway (Highway 99) corridor from Howe Sound fjord through the Coast Mountains: granite walls (the Stawamus Chief), glacier-fed rivers, and alpine terrain around Whistler and Pemberton.
Sea-to-Sky.economy: Whistler Blackcomb (North America's largest ski resort) drives a four-season tourism economy; Squamish is one of BC's fastest-growing municipalities with an outdoor-recreation and commuter economy; Pemberton anchors farming (famous seed potatoes).
Sea-to-Sky.building_stock: Alpine chalets and lodges built for snow load, resort strata condos in Whistler, and new family subdivisions in Squamish; Pemberton mixes farm properties and new development.

Okanagan.climate: Semi-arid interior climate: hot dry summers regularly topping 35°C, cold but comparatively dry winters, and one of Canada's longest growing seasons; wildfire smoke and interface fire risk are annual realities; irrigation is essential.
Okanagan.terrain: A chain of long lakes (Okanagan, Skaha, Osoyoos) in a dry valley between the Thompson Plateau and Monashee ranges; benchland terraces of orchards and vineyards above the lakeshore.
Okanagan.economy: Canada's premier wine region (180+ wineries) and historic orchard belt; Kelowna is BC's largest interior city with UBC Okanagan, Kelowna International Airport, tech and healthcare; tourism and retirement migration drive growth.
Okanagan.building_stock: Lakeshore and view homes, vineyard and orchard acreages, fast-built post-2000 suburbs (West Kelowna, Lake Country), and older single-storey homes in Penticton and Vernon; stucco and dry-climate exteriors dominate.

Thompson-Nicola & Cariboo.climate: Dry interior continental climate: hot summers (Kamloops and Lytton record some of Canada's highest temperatures), cold winters, low precipitation; grassland wildfire risk and freeze-thaw cycles define exterior maintenance.
Thompson-Nicola & Cariboo.terrain: Sagebrush grasslands and hoodoos where the North and South Thompson rivers meet at Kamloops, rising to the Cariboo Plateau's ranchland and lakes country further north.
Thompson-Nicola & Cariboo.economy: Ranching country (the historic Cariboo Gold Rush trail), mining (Highland Valley Copper near Logan Lake is Canada's largest copper mine), forestry, and transport — Kamloops sits at the junction of the Trans-Canada and Yellowhead highways with Thompson Rivers University.
Thompson-Nicola & Cariboo.building_stock: Ranch houses and acreages, post-war grid neighbourhoods in Kamloops and Williams Lake, lakefront cabins, and manufactured homes in rural areas.

Northern BC - Prince George.climate: Continental northern climate: long cold winters to -30°C, short warm summers; deep frost, ice damming and snow-load engineering are standard; spring breakup roughens roads and sites.
Northern BC - Prince George.terrain: Rolling Interior Plateau forest at the confluence of the Fraser and Nechako rivers; Prince George is the hub of a vast forested hinterland.
Northern BC - Prince George.economy: Prince George is northern BC's capital in all but name: forestry and pulp mills, the University of Northern British Columbia (UNBC), regional healthcare, and CN Rail's junction of north-south and east-west lines.
Northern BC - Prince George.building_stock: Post-war and 1970s-boom houses built for deep winter, newer subdivisions on the city's benches, and rural properties on large lots outside town.

Kootenays.climate: Interior mountain climate with heavy snowfall (the Powder Highway ski belt), wet-cold winters and warm summers; steep freeze-thaw cycling and snow-shed engineering; lake-moderated pockets around Nelson and Creston are gentler.
Kootenays.terrain: Parallel north-south ranges (Selkirks, Purcells, Monashees, Rockies) separated by long lakes (Kootenay, Arrow, Slocan) and river valleys; communities cling to lakeshores and valley floors.
Kootenays.economy: Teck's smelter at Trail (one of the world's largest zinc-lead smelters), mining heritage towns, forestry, hydro dams on the Columbia, ski tourism (Revelstoke, Fernie, Rossland), and Nelson's arts economy.
Kootenays.building_stock: Some of BC's best-preserved heritage streetscapes (Nelson, Rossland, Fernie), miners' cottages, lakefront cabins, and mountain homes built for metres of snow.

Peace Region & Northeast.climate: BC's only true prairie-continental climate, east of the Rockies: winters can hit -40°C with chinook swings, summers are long-daylight and warm; permafrost-free but deep-frost construction rules apply.
Peace Region & Northeast.terrain: The Peace River country: rolling farmland (BC's only large-scale grain belt) cut by the deep Peace River valley, with the Rockies' foothills to the west and boreal muskeg north of Fort Nelson.
Peace Region & Northeast.economy: Natural gas (the Montney formation makes Fort St. John BC's energy capital), the Site C dam project, grain and canola farming around Dawson Creek — Mile 0 of the Alaska Highway — and forestry.
Peace Region & Northeast.building_stock: Farmhouses, energy-boom subdivisions in Fort St. John and Dawson Creek, worker housing, and rural shops/quonsets; buildings engineered for extreme cold.

Northern BC - Northwest.climate: Coastal-to-interior gradient: Prince Rupert is one of Canada's wettest and cloudiest cities (about 2,600 mm of rain a year), while Terrace and the Bulkley Valley inland get real winters with heavy snow; Haida Gwaii is mild, wet and wind-battered.
Northern BC - Northwest.terrain: The Skeena and Bulkley river valleys cutting through the Coast Mountains to the North Coast fjords; Haida Gwaii archipelago offshore; vast wilderness north along the Stewart-Cassiar Highway.
Northern BC - Northwest.economy: The Port of Prince Rupert (North America's closest port to Asia, a fast-growing container terminal), LNG Canada at Kitimat (Canada's largest private-sector construction project), Rio Tinto's aluminium smelter, salmon fishing on the Skeena, and Indigenous-led economies including Haida Gwaii tourism.
Northern BC - Northwest.building_stock: Rain-screened coastal housing in Prince Rupert, company-town stock in Kitimat, valley homesteads around Smithers and Terrace, and remote off-grid properties; marine-grade materials matter on the coast.
